chunk_summary: The four main impact areas for SaaS GTM in 2026: 1) Target Market — define best-fit buyers using real buying behavior, psychographics (role, how they're measured, values), not just demographics. Buying decisions are now about safety, not just better/faster/cheaper. AI helps by analyzing CRM data to identify which roles and problems convert fastest. 2) Circle of Influence — be known by the right people before they're ready to buy; obscurity is the enemy. Post high-value free content consistently on social media (LinkedIn's new algorithm prioritizes comments and engagement). Test organic content, then amplify what works with paid ads. 3) Relationships — guide buyers through uncertainty by helping them make sense of the problem, justify internally, and feel safe saying yes. Provide a 'how to buy' document so customers understand the process. 4) Offer — create offers that move buyers into meaningful conversations, not free trials. Sell today, educate tomorrow. Monetize the sales process by bringing a paid discovery/diagnostic forward instead of giving free consulting.
content: First of all I'm going to talk about the four main impact areas in your business. ... So first of all your target market. So your target market is really about not just you know who you want to sell to but actually identifying the best fit buyers based on real buying behavior. ... Real buying behavior, commercial urgency, and the problems that you're best positioned to solve. So when this is clear, when you get absolutely dialed in on your target market, each the lead generation becomes simpler, sales cycles shorten and the conversion rates and the conversations become better. ... So I want you to think about that is who do you sell to? Not just the demographics but the psychographics. Who are the customers you really want to sell to? ... a lot of purchases these days are no longer based on necessarily the best but actually what is going to make the person who's making the buying decision safe. So buying decisions now are not necessarily better faster or cheaper ... it's important to think about what's going to make sure the customer who's buying the product look good. ... So you're covering their backside. ... is your product not only a good choice, but is it a safe choice? ... Then it goes to the second part which is your circle of influence. How do you ... be known by the right people before they're ready to buy. That's a key indicator there before they're ready to buy. ... So your goal is not reach or impressions but constant visibility with your best buyers. So trust is already established when sales conversation starts. ... the most successful, the people who are really growing super successful businesses right now today are the ones posting the most amount of stuff onto social media. And it's got to be high value. ... So put out free content and then what you do with that free content is you run paid ads based on that free content. Find stuff that actually is indexed well. Find stuff that actually the market has responded to for free organically. Then run advertising behind that ... The third part of the four keys is your relationships. So we need to build on and maintain trust through the buying journey. ... the real job of building a relationship is to guide buyers through uncertainty. ... They haven't bought your thing before. They don't know if it's any good. So we want to help them make sense of the problem. ... Help them justify the decision internally. ... also you want to make them feel safe saying yes ... what we developed for them is a way to guide customers in how to buy. ... provide them a document this is the process in which people normally go through to make a purchasing decision ... And the last part here is your offer. ... So in B2B SAS and software, we need to be thinking about creating a clear offer that moves buyers into meaningful conversations. ... Strong offers reduces risk for the buyer. ... we sell today, we educate tomorrow. We get really really narrow and really focused on the exact issue we're dealing with. So the role of the offer is to progress the sale, not to give everything away. ... what we do is we bring forward a piece of the work that you would normally do as the onboarding. You bring that forward to create confidence in the sale. ... what we know about business and buyers is they will always buy consulting far quicker than they will buy software because buying consulting is a different budget.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the four impact areas for SaaS GTM in 2026?
Why is buyer safety more important than better/faster/cheaper?
How should I structure my offer instead of free trials?
What does 'sell today, educate tomorrow' mean?

chunk_summary: The evolution of sales: In 1999, salespeople had 4-8 phone calls to build trust, diagnose, prescribe, and close. In 2026, marketing does 80% of that work before a sales call ever happens — prospects consume content, read reviews, check LinkedIn, and only then engage. Salespeople now get ONE call and must be much better at instant rapport, qualification, diagnosis, and prescription — not crushing objections. Growth happens at the speed trust is built and transferred.
content: So, the evolution of sales is something you really got to take notice of right now. So, back in 1999 when I first started my first tech business ... we put an advert that was 5 centimeters high by one inch wide in the newspaper ... people would phone our 1-800 number ... and then the salesperson had multiple opportunities to build a relationship. They built trust they built credibility they told the story of the company they did qualification of the customer, their qualification of themselves. They did diagnosis, they did prescription, they introduced people to existing customers, they did all of this work over the series of four, five, eight phone calls ... The problem is now in 2026 and beyond ... marketing happens they become problem or product aware then what happens is they go out there into the internet they look around they see things that's available they read reviews They consume your content. They might see you on Insta. They might see you on Facebook. They might see you on LinkedIn. They might subscribe to your newsletter. They might download your ebook or something like that. So, they're going to do a whole bunch of things before they engage with sales. Now, that doesn't mean that sales people become less important. Actually, sales people have to be much much better than they ever have been because that phone call is real stress for the customer. They don't want to talk to a salesperson. So therefore the salesperson has to be really well trained, really good at building instant rapport and really have a great understanding of the problems the customer's going through. ... it's about qualification, diagnosis, and prescription. That's when you can actually move into a sales conversation. So, marketing is doing a lot more work these days. Sales people just got to be a lot better these days. ... growth happens at the speed trust is built. And so the more trust you can create, the faster and the faster you can transfer it, the faster you'll grow.
questions_on_this_chunk: How has B2B SaaS sales changed since the 1990s?
Why do salespeople need to be better in 2026?
What should a modern sales call focus on?
How does marketing replace early sales conversations?

chunk_summary: How to build and transfer trust at scale: video testimonials are king. Create a 'What Clients Say' document — a book of testimonials so thorough that prospects stop asking for reference calls. Get customers on camera and paper. Reviews, case studies, and specific outcome metrics ('increased bookings 150% with no extra staff') serve as proof that makes you the safe choice. Trust allows you to charge premium prices even against bigger competitors with worse products.
content: So what do you do? First of all, here's some examples. Video testimonials are king of the hill. And when you can get a customer to say things like, 'There's my customer work. Workers improve your outcomes and process for EVO energy directly to Evo Energy is like the second or third biggest energy provider in Australia.' ... Another thing you can use here is examples and case studies. So Medi law increases weekly bookings by 150% with Nexusoft implying no extra staff. When you send that to another medical legal organization go here's what we've done for somebody like you. They go I get it. I understand the problem. I understand the solution. Let's talk more. ... This is what I do before anybody engages with tech talk. I send them this document called what clients say. It's the 25 page book of testimonials. Literally ... they scroll through. They almost get tired of reading testimonials. By me implementing that, not a single person in the last two years has asked to speak to a resistant customer because they feel like the only reason somebody wants to talk to an existing customer is because they don't have enough evidence to take to somebody else that you're a good supplier. So your job right now is to get testimonials and reviews. Put them into a book. find a way to actually get them on camera ... get them on camera get them on paper and that's how you're going to build that trust because the speed in which you can grow your business is the speed in which you can build and transfer trust.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the best type of social proof for B2B SaaS?
How do I stop prospects asking for reference calls?
What should a 'What Clients Say' document include?
How do testimonials help charge premium prices?

chunk_summary: The complete GTM blueprint cycle: 1) Get clear on target market (psychographics, not just demographics). 2) Develop high-impact content addressing buyer pain points. 3) Create lead magnets (not just ebooks — comment-to-DM automations, blueprints, guides; the sniff test: would my client want this?). 4) Distribute organically first, then amplify winners with paid ads and retargeting. 5) Invest in SEO/SEM optimized for AI search (ChatGPT is launching ad-supported tiers — early adopters will benefit from low supply/high demand). 6) Build partner networks, affiliate programs, and community participation. 7) Optimize continuously — run more experiments. The people who experiment most win most.
content: Here's the sales and marketing blueprint. Really complex slide. ... this is about bringing everything together for you in one spot. So, get really clear on your target market. And we've spoken about that. It's not just demographics, it's psychographics. It's finding people who really are right in your sweet spot. The second part is developing high impact content ... develop content that directly addresses your buyer's pain points providing value and positioning your solution as the answer. The third part is lead magnets. Now lead magnets in the past have just been our guide to or our white paper or our ebook. But today lead magnets I see these really effectively happening out there for clients is ... you know we've had 14,000 interactions with clients. We've found a way to solve this problem. Would you like our blueprint? It's a one page blueprint. You know type in blueprint down below and people can send that. ... That flows into distribution. Obviously organic and paid. Organic is king. Paid is when the organic starts working. ... Retargeting, re-engage your visitors if they land on your website. there's enough technology out there to identify who's come onto your website and then find them and follow them around the internet and engage in that conversation. The third part is SEO and SEM. So SEO and SEM these days is really about making sure that when you are publishing articles, you're publishing secondary articles as well that are AI enabled because AI search is a real thing. for example, open AI people from chat GPT are now going to start offering a lower price level with ads. ... So if you're keeping your eye on the marketplace here, you want to think about how you can actually get those out there and start working that system. So it's always supply and demand. So build your network. ... we got to make sure we partner with industry influencers, participate in relevant communities, and expand your reach. Once again, that builds your credibility, which cycles back to trust because it's all about trust. And the last part there is optimize. See the cycle, understand it, add in whatever AI tool you're using, and then run that cycle again. The more experiments you run, the more successful you'll be. ... the people that experiment more are the ones that win more. ... when you're distributing ads, when you're putting content out there, put lots of content out there in different ways.
questions_on_this_chunk: What does the complete SaaS GTM blueprint look like?
How should lead magnets change in 2026?
Why should I care about ChatGPT launching ads?
How many marketing experiments should I run?
